payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

payload.message: 速度一般、质量一般、空余时间有的时候多有的时候少。

**适配判断**
- 是否适合六问法：适合
- 问题类型：差距暂不清楚的行动类问题
- 判断理由：目标已经能落到当前工作表现，但现状还比较粗

- 当前在第几步：第 2 问，现状是什么
- 这一步为什么要做：第三问要算差距，前提是现状不能只停在“还行、一般”，要尽量变成可比较的事实

**暂定理解**
- 我目前的理解：你现在的状态大致是
- 速度一般
- 质量一般
- 空余时间不稳定，有时多有时少
- 这还是暂定版：方向有了，但还不够具体，暂时还不能进入第三问

**回答方向**
- 速度：比如一项常规任务通常多久做完，和你理想中相比慢在哪里
- 质量：比如经常返工、需要别人补、领导会指出哪些问题
- 空余时间：比如一周里大概有几天能比较从容，几天会被任务推着走

**请你回答**
先只回答一个最容易量化的维度就行：  
你现在在工作里，最典型的一类任务，通常要花多久做完？你理想中希望缩短到多久？

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)

- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.

- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.

- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.

- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.

- 空余时间上，你觉得目前没有明显差距，所以这一轮可以先不作为主分析对象

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments: {"command":"Select-String -Path \"docs\\method\\界定问题六问法.md\" -Pattern \"第 3 问后的第一次分流判断|重新界定真正的问题|重开规则|必须开启新一轮六问法|原始问题可能不成立\" -Encoding utf8 | ForEach-Object { $_.LineNumber.ToString() + ': ' + $_.Line }","workdir":"D:\\00容器\\yc\\yc_think","timeout_ms":20000}

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments: {"command":"$path = (Resolve-Path -LiteralPath 'sources/raw/界定问题六问法.docx').Path; Add-Type -AssemblyName System.IO.Compression.FileSystem; $zip = [System.IO.Compression.ZipFile]::OpenRead($path); try { $entry = $zip.Entries | Where-Object { $_.FullName -eq 'word/document.xml' }; $reader = New-Object System.IO.StreamReader($entry.Open()); $xml = $reader.ReadToEnd(); $reader.Close(); $xml = [regex]::Replace($xml, '</w:p>', \"`n\"); $xml = [regex]::Replace($xml, '<[^>]+>', ''); $xml = [System.Net.WebUtility]::HtmlDecode($xml); $lines = $xml -split \"`n\" | ForEach-Object { $_.Trim() } | Where-Object { $_ }; for ($i=0; $i -lt $lines.Length; $i++) { if ($lines[$i] -match '通过第三问|第四到第六个问题|重新界定后的问题|万一重新界定后的问题|如果没有差距的话|差距和现状的描述要与目标一一对应') { '{0}: {1}' -f ($i+1), $lines[$i] } } } finally { $zip.Dispose() }","workdir":"D:\\00容器\\yc\\yc_think","timeout_ms":20000}

payload.message:
严格说，原版六问法在第三问后：

可以“改写待分析问题 / 重开一轮”，但还不算正式“重新界定真正的问题”。

这两个动作要分开看。

根据整理稿，[界定问题六问法.md](/D:/00容器/yc/yc_think/docs/method/界定问题六问法.md#L356) 明确写了“第 3 问后的第一次分流判断”：
- 如果没有差距
- 差距不在原提问上
- 原提问只是情绪表达
- 目标和现状不在同一维度

这时要做两件事：
- 写出原问题为什么不成立
- 生成一个新的待分析问题，再从第 1 问重新开始

但“重新界定真正的问题”作为正式收口，是放在后面的单独一节，[界定问题六问法.md](/D:/00容器/yc/yc_think/docs/method/界定问题六问法.md#L593) 写的是：把第 6 问结果改写成最终可执行的问题。

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

39: 第3问差距是什么
40: 完成前两问后，就到第三问了。
41: 问题来源于差距，目标有了、现状也有了，差距就出来了，问题也就有了。
42: 如果没有差距的话，是不存在任何问题的。比如说解决“贫穷”的问题，可能你认为年收入不足10万的人是贫穷的，有一个人的年收入只有6万，因此他就有贫穷的问题要去解决。但如果他的金钱欲望只有5万一年，那么就没有所谓“贫穷”的问题要去解决了，因为根本就没有差距。
43: 还是回到朋友觉得没有发展前途的问题上来，目标是3万元一个月，现状是1.4万元一个月，差距很明显就是1.6万元一个月。
44: 第4问目标背后的目的
45: 通过第三问，确定差距后，我们是不是直接帮朋友想各种方法，弥补每月1.6万元的收入差距就可以了呢？
46: 还不行！
47: 在真正动手去解决这个1.6万元的差距之前，你还要再继续问三个问题，也就是第四到第六个问题，以确定这个差距是否是真正的问题。
48: 第四个问题，你要用探究问题本质的方法，用5why追问朋友为什么要月薪达到3万元。你可以这么问： 你为什么想要加薪到3万元呢？
49: 他可能会说，因为我准备买房，每月房贷要近2万，如果不影响现在的生活质量的话，起码要加薪到月薪3万元。
50: 根据他的这个回答，你可以接着问为什么： 为什么一定要通过加薪，而不是其它方式去弥补收入呢？
51: 通过不断地问为什么，直至你认为已经触及到问题的本质为止。
52: 问第四个问题的原因是去确定朋友这个月薪3万元的目标是不是合理的。比如说，他要月薪3万元的目的是因为他有个同事最近加薪到2.5万了，他认为自己水平比同事高，至少应该月薪3万。如果背后是这个原因的话，那这个目标可能就不合理了。
53: 第5问造成现状的原因
54: 通过第四个问题确定了目标背后的目的后，还要继续问第五个问题，以确定造成当前现状的原因是什么，也就是为什么朋友当前的月薪是1.4万元。
55: 比如，你可以这么问： 你目前的月薪是1.4万元，跟你条件相似的同事，也差不多是这个薪资，还是比这个薪资高呢？
56: 这么问的目的是想验证朋友目前的薪资状况是因为公司整体工资水平影响的，还是因为他个人的原因导致未达预期的。
---
89: 分析出导致差距的原因后，你就可以重新界定问题了，界定的方法也是2种。
90: 1）如果是目标不合理导致的，那就调整目标；
91: 2）如果是现状未改变导致的，那就从造成现状的原因入手加以改变。万一导致现象的原因难以改变，也就是现状是由客观因素造成的，比如任何物体的速度都不能超过光速；或者对现状的改变超过了你目前的能力和资源，比如要你徒手举起一个重达一吨的小轿车，那就将导致该现状的原因作为解决问题的限制因素加以考虑。
92: 我们看回“ 我觉得继续呆在公司没有发展前途了，应该怎么办呢？ ”这个问题，看看当时重新界定它的背后思路是怎样的。
93: 1）增加每月收入到3万元的目标是合理的，但限制为薪资收入是不合理的；
94: 2）1.4万元的薪资现状是难以改变的，原因是行业和公司薪酬制度的限制，因此要将这个原因作为将来解决问题的限制因素；
95: 因此，重新界定的问题就是：如何在本职工作加薪困难的条件下，每月增加1.6万的收入。
96: 第六个注意点：一个问题做多遍六问法的界定是正常的
97: 万一重新界定后的问题，自己去动手解决时发现依然是差距不明确的问题怎么办呢？方法很简单，再来一遍界定问题六问法的过程。
98: 你在刚开始练习六问法时，出现这种情况是很正常的，多练习几遍就好了。

payload.arguments: {"command":"$path = (Resolve-Path -LiteralPath 'sources/raw/六问法示例1：2个月内优化业务流程，提升团队工作效率20_.xmind').Path; Add-Type -AssemblyName System.IO.Compression.FileSystem; $zip = [System.IO.Compression.ZipFile]::OpenRead($path); try { $entry = $zip.Entries | Where-Object { $_.FullName -eq 'content.xml' }; $reader = New-Object System.IO.StreamReader($entry.Open()); $xml = $reader.ReadToEnd(); $reader.Close(); $xml = [regex]::Replace($xml, '><', \">`n<\"); $lines = $xml -split \"`n\"; $patterns = '差距是什么','目标的背后目的是什么','那现在想解决的问题是哪一个','重新界定真正的问题'; foreach($pattern in $patterns){ \"--- PATTERN: $pattern ---\"; $matches = $lines | Select-String -Pattern $pattern -SimpleMatch; foreach($m in $matches | Select-Object -First 8){ $idx = $m.LineNumber - 1; $start=[Math]::Max(0,$idx-2); $end=[Math]::Min($lines.Length-1,$idx+3); for($i=$start;$i -le $end;$i++){ $lines[$i] } ; '---' } } } finally { $zip.Dispose() }","workdir":"D:\\00容器\\yc\\yc_think","timeout_ms":20000}

payload.message:
你说得对，刚才我不够严格。  
这次我只按 `sources/raw` 里的原始文件判断，不用整理稿。

只看原始文件，我的结论是：

1. 原版六问法在第三问**不正式重新界定问题**。  
在原始 [界定问题六问法.docx](/D:/00容器/yc/yc_think/sources/raw/界定问题六问法.docx) 里，第三问对应的原话是：
- “问题来源于差距，目标有了、现状也有了，差距就出来了，问题也就有了。”
- “如果没有差距的话，是不存在任何问题的。”
- “通过第三问，确定差距后……还要再继续问三个问题，也就是第四到第六个问题，以确定这个差距是否是真正的问题。”

这说明第三问的职责是：
- 确定差距
- 判断有没有差距

但不是在第三问就正式把“真正的问题”改写出来。

2. 原版里，**新问题/真正要解决的问题的切换，主要落在第四问以后**。  
我刚开了原始 [六问法示例1：2个月内优化业务流程，提升团队工作效率20_.xmind](/D:/00容器/yc/yc_think/sources/raw/六问法示例1：2个月内优化业务流程，提升团队工作效率20_.xmind)，里面：
- “差距是什么？”是单独一个节点
- “目标的背后目的是什么？”是下一层
- “那现在想解决的问题是哪一个？”出现在第四问下面
- “重新界定真正的问题”出现在后面单独一段

所以从 raw 示例看也一样：
- 第三问先定差距
- 第四问才开始切“真正要解决的是哪个问题”
- 最终“重新界定真正的问题”是在更后面收口

3. 所以，按 raw 原版更严谨的说法是：
- 第三问最多只能说：`当前明确存在差距的是哪个维度`
- 如果根本没有差距，就说明这个问题不成立
- 但不能在第三问就把“真正的问题”正式重写出来
- 更不能把“换工作是不是方案”这种判断提前到第三问

也就是说，你刚才那个提醒是对的。  
按 raw 原版，我们在你这个例子里，第三问此刻最多只能得到：

- 空余时间维度：目前看没有明显差距
- 质量维度：存在差距

更原版一点，还不能马上说“那新问题就是……”。

如果你愿意，我现在就按 **raw 原版边界** 接着带你，只把第三问做完整，不提前进入第四问。  
那下一句最合适的问题会是：

**如果只看质量这个维度，你想达到的标准，和你现在的实际水平之间，具体差在哪里？**

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

content.text: 你给我点思路呢，这里我一是没有很对的感觉。

payload.message: 你给我点思路呢，这里我一是没有很对的感觉。

有这种“不太对”的感觉很正常，通常说明这一步里有两个尺子混在一起了。

你现在手里其实有两套标准：

- 一套是工作标准：`领导满意、不返工`
- 一套是外部参照标准：`像现在流行短视频那样好`

这两套标准不一定是同一个东西。  
而第三问只做一件事：**先把差距放到同一把尺子上。**

所以你现在可以先不要问：
- 为什么达不到
- 是不是该换工作
- 背后真正想要什么

先只选一种“质量尺子”。

**我给你 3 个思路，你选最有感觉的一个就行：**

1. 用“返工”当尺子  
差距就写成：
- 我希望大多数 PPT/视频一次能过
- 但现在多数任务都要返工

2. 用“领导认可的完成标准”当尺子  
差距就写成：
- 我希望做出来的内容能达到领导认可的标准
- 但现在做出来的内容还经常达不到这个标准

3. 用“内容表现力”当尺子  
差距就写成：
- 我希望 PPT/视频在结构、表达、吸引力上更成熟
- 但现在作品在这些方面还比较弱

如果按原版六问法、又尽量贴近你当前工作场景，我更建议你先用第 `1` 或第 `2` 个，因为它们和“当前工作是否做好”更同维度。

**请你回答**
这三个里面，哪一个最接近你现在真正感觉到的“质量差距”？  
你可以直接回：`1`、`2`、`3`，或者自己改一句。

- 我希望做出来的内容能达到领导认可的标准
- 但现在做出来的内容还经常达不到这个标准

3. 用“内容表现力”当尺子  
差距就写成：
- 我希望 PPT/视频在结构、表达、吸引力上更成熟
- 但现在作品在这些方面还比较弱

如果按原版六问法、又尽量贴近你当前工作场景，我更建议你先用第 `1` 或第 `2` 个，因为它们和“当前工作是否做好”更同维度。

**请你回答**
这三个里面，哪一个最接近你现在真正感觉到的“质量差距”？  
你可以直接回：`1`、`2`、`3`，或者自己改一句。

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

content.text: 3，因为这里做好了前面2个自然解决了撒，或者部分解决了吧。

payload.message: 3，因为这里做好了前面2个自然解决了撒，或者部分解决了吧。

这个选择有感觉，而且我能理解你为什么选 `3`。

你真正卡的，可能不是“返工”这个表面现象，而是更底层的质量感：
- 作品不够成熟
- 表达力不够
- 和你心里想达到的内容水准有落差

但按原版第三问，我想帮你守住一个边界：

**可以先把 `3` 作为质量差距的表达方向，但先不要用“3 做好了，1 和 2 自然解决”来收缩。**  
因为这句已经有一点后面“因果判断”的味道了，不完全是第三问。

所以第三问更稳的写法可以先变成：

- 质量上的差距是：我希望自己做出来的 `PPT` 和宣传视频，在结构、表达和整体完成度上，达到更成熟、更有传播力的水准；
- 但现在做出来的内容，在这些方面还不够，所以多数任务会返工。

你下一步最适合做的，不是再选“哪个最核心”，而是把这个质量差距再拆开一点。  
我给你几个可选维度，你看哪个最贴近：

- 结构：内容逻辑、页与页/段与段的组织
- 表达：文案、标题、重点提炼、说服力
- 视觉：版式、美感、镜头感、节奏感
- 传播感：像不像现在能打动人的短视频/宣传内容
- AI使用：会不会用 AI 产出更好的初稿、素材、脚本、分镜

你先不用全答。  
只说 2-3 个你觉得“现在最明显不够”的点就行。

content.text: 视觉、传播感、AI使用

payload.message: 视觉、传播感、AI使用

很好，这一下就顺了。

按 raw 原版的第三问边界，我们现在可以先把它理解成：

- 这轮真正存在差距的，是**质量维度**
- 而这个质量维度里，你目前最明显的差距，落在 3 个方面：
- 视觉
- 传播感
- AI 使用

这里先不急着解释原因，也先不判断“哪个最根本”。  
第三问先做到“差距列清”就已经很够了。

所以如果按更贴近原版的方式，你现在这一轮第三问可以先暂定成这样：

- 目标端：希望做出的 `PPT` 和宣传视频更成熟，更有传播力，更能支持当前工作做好
- 现状端：目前多数相关任务仍要返工
- 差距端：当前质量差距主要体现在 `视觉`、`传播感`、`AI 使用` 这三个方面

如果再往原版第三问收一步，我会建议你把这 3 个差距各写成半句话。比如：

- 视觉：现在作品的版式/镜头/整体呈现感还不够成熟
- 传播感：现在内容还不够像能抓住人的宣传内容
- AI 使用：现在还没有把 AI 真正用成提升作品质量的稳定能力

这样第三问就比较完整了。

再往下，按原版六问法，下一步才该进入第四问。  
第四问不是问“为什么做不好”，而是问：

**你为什么想达到这个质量目标？**

也就是：
- 你为什么这么在意“更成熟、更有传播力”
- 这背后真正想解决的是什么

如果你愿意，我下一步就按 raw 原版，正式带你进第四问。

这样第三问就比较完整了。

再往下，按原版六问法，下一步才该进入第四问。  
第四问不是问“为什么做不好”，而是问：

**你为什么想达到这个质量目标？**

也就是：
- 你为什么这么在意“更成熟、更有传播力”
- 这背后真正想解决的是什么

如果你愿意，我下一步就按 raw 原版，正式带你进第四问。

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

23: 我给你提炼了四个5why的使用要点，可以帮你很好地避免以上5个错误，真正用好5why。

34: 2.1 用0.5why问出正确的“为什么”
37: 如果你能在问下一层的“为什么”之前做个验证——也就是0.5why——的话，你就能大大提高问与答的质量了。
40: 在第二个why——“为什么害怕领导质疑自己的工作能力”——之前，你先做一个0.5why验证：【没听明白再问领导】一定会导致【领导质疑自己的工作能力】吗？
46: 这个0.5why用起来很简单，就是用原因来反向验证是否必然会产生当前现象或问题。它的验证范式是“ 【xxx原因】一定会导致【xxx现象或问题】吗？ ”。
50: 这两类问题，都适用0.5why以问出正确的“为什么”。
58: 但如果你采用0.5why的方法来验证每一个“为什么”的回答的话，就不会失焦了，而且效率更高。
59: 比如，在开始问“为什么买奶茶的客人少了”之前，你先用0.5why做反向验证，“【买奶茶的客人少了】一定会导致【这个月销售额就比上月下降4万元】吗？”。
61: 图4-1 0.5why法应用示例1

65: 接下来我们继续看一个0.5why方法在识别初步解决方案以探寻真正的问题或目的上的应用。
71: 但如果你运用0.5why来做验证的话，就能更快地确定提问者的目标是否只是初步解决方案，或者是否合理。
72: 比如，在开始第二问“为什么跟主管处不来”之前，你可以先做一个0.5why验证： 【跟主管处不来】就一定要【这个月从公司离职】吗？ 。如下图所示。
73: 图5-1 0.5why法应用示例2
75: 经过这个0.5why验证之后，我们就可以继续往下确认：
78: 如果是情况一的话，那我们就继续用第二问来探究提问者真正的目的或想解决的问题，并用同样的方法做0.5why验证。如下图所示。
79: 图5-2 0.5why法应用示例2
81: 图5-3 0.5why法应用示例2
82: 以上就是0.5why法分别在两类不同问题上的应用：
84: 验证范式：【回答（原因）】一定会导致【提问（现象/问题）】
86: 验证范式：【回答】就一定要【提问】
87: 如果你细心的话，你会发现在“没听明白也不敢再问领导”的这个案例中，0.5why的用法是： 【没听明白再问领导】一定会导致【领导质疑自己的工作能力】吗？
88: 而不是 【 害怕领导质疑自己的工作能力 】一定会导致【  没听明白也不敢再问领导 】吗？
90: 图6 0.5why法识别因果倒置的逻辑错误
91: 2.2 用0.5why检验问答的逻辑错误
92: 其实，0.5why除了可以帮你问出更准确的“为什么”之外，还能帮你发现逻辑跳跃、问答死循环的逻辑错误。
93: 比如开头案例的第三问是 “为什么害怕失去领导的信任，因为害怕对职业发展产生不良影响”，这一问用0.5why检验的话，就会变成“为什么失去领导的信任，就会对职业发展产生不良影响?”，你很明显地就会发现其中的逻辑跳跃了。
94: 还有这个案例中的第四问是“为什么害怕对职业发展产生不良影响？因为想得到更好的职业发展”，用0.5why检验的话，就会变成“为什么对职业发展产生不良影响，就会影响职业发展”，你一下子就能发现这个问答死循环的错误。
95: 因此，只要你能用好0.5why就能有效避免三大常犯错误：
116: 发散完成后再做收敛。收敛的时候一定要做好分层分组，并且每一组都要按逻辑顺序归纳，你可以遵循的逻辑顺序有时间顺序、结构顺序、重要性顺序以及演绎顺序。
144: 但如果你能先准确描述问题，学会使用0.5why方法和框架，再熟记问到刚刚好的提问清单，那么对你而言，5why就变得既简单、又能真正发挥效用。

payload.message:
我刚刚专门只看了 `sources/raw` 里的原始文件，结论可以比较明确地说：

按 raw 原文，第四问和 `0.5Why` 的关系是成立的，而且第四问里确实已经在用“0.5Why 那个方向”的验证，只是原始 `界定问题六问法.docx` 里还没有统一写成 `0.5Why` 这个术语。

只基于 raw，我给你拆成 4 点：

1. 第四问的核心职责是什么  
原始 [界定问题六问法.docx](/D:/00容器/yc/yc_think/sources/raw/界定问题六问法.docx) 写得很直接：
- “第4问目标背后的目的”
- “问第四个问题的原因是去确定朋友这个月薪3万元的目标是不是合理的。”
- “绝大多数情况下，我们或他人设定的目标往往是某个问题的初步解决方案，因此需要用5why多多追问目标背后的目的，以抓到真正的目标。”

所以第四问的核心不是找技能原因，不是找执行原因，而是：
- 判断当前目标是不是合理
- 判断当前目标是不是某个“初步解决方案”
- 往下追到真正目标

2. 第四问在 raw 原文里怎么问  
原始 `docx` 给的典型问法是：
- “你为什么想要加薪到3万元呢？”
- “为什么一定要通过加薪，而不是其它方式去弥补收入呢？”

这第二句其实已经非常接近后来整理里的第四问 `0.5Why` 范式了。  
也就是说，第四问的 raw 原始动作本来就是两步：
- 先 `why`
- 再问“为什么一定要通过这个目标，而不是别的方式”

3. `0.5Why` 在 raw 里到底怎么定义  
原始 [真正用好5why（图文版）.docx](/D:/00容器/yc/yc_think/sources/raw/真正用好5why（图文版）.docx) 里明确写了两种用法：

第一种，用在原因分析上：
- 验证范式：`【回答（原因）】一定会导致【提问（现象/问题）】吗？`

第二种，用在识别“初步解决方案 / 真正目的”上：
- 验证范式：`【回答】就一定要【提问】`

而且它给的例子就是：
- `【跟主管处不来】就一定要【这个月从公司离职】吗？`

这和第四问完全一致。

4. raw 示例里第四问怎么落地  
原始示例对应的处理稿也能印证这一点：
在“优化流程”的例子里，第四问下面就是：
- 先问：`为什么【两个月内优化业务流程，将团队工作效率提升20%】呢？`
- 再做 1.5why：`【某个回答】就一定要【两个月内优化业务流程，将团队工作效率提升20%】吗？`
- 然后才出现：`那现在想解决的问题是哪一个？`

所以按 raw 来看，流程非常清楚：
- 第三问：只定差距
- 第四问：先问目标背后的目的
- 然后在第四问里用“【回答】就一定要【提问】吗？”这种 `0.5Why` 验证
- 再判断是不是要切换到新的真正目标/问题

所以，回到我们刚才那轮对话，如果要更贴 raw 原版，第四问不该问成：
- “如果你已经能用 AI 来完成工作了，那真正解决了什么？”

这句虽然方向不算错，但不够 raw。

更贴 raw 的问法应该是先这样：

1. `为什么你想把 PPT 和宣传视频做到更成熟、更有传播力，也更会用 AI？`
2. 如果你给出一个回答，再继续：
   `【你的这个回答】就一定要【把 PPT 和宣传视频做到更成熟、更有传播力，也更会用 AI】吗？`

这才更像 raw 原版第四问。

如果你愿意，我现在就按 raw 原版第四问重新带你，不掺后来的整理说法。  
那第一句就会是：

**为什么你想把 PPT 和宣传视频做到更成熟、更有传播力，也更会用 AI？**

- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

payload.message: 你可以结合我回答的是的，来帮我判断一下是否有问题呢，还是说这个因人而异。

结合你刚才这个“是的”，我的判断是：

**大概率有问题，不是完全不能这么答，但这句“是的”现在答得过满了。**

原因不是“因人而异就都行”，而是 raw 原版第四问的验证，不是看你主观上认不认同，而是看这条路径是不是**真的必要**。

原始依据在这两份 raw 文件里：
[界定问题六问法.docx](/D:/00容器/yc/yc_think/sources/raw/界定问题六问法.docx)
[真正用好5why（图文版）.docx](/D:/00容器/yc/yc_think/sources/raw/真正用好5why（图文版）.docx)

raw 的意思其实很明确：
- 第四问要判断“当前目标是不是初步解决方案”
- `0.5Why` 的这一类验证范式是：`【回答】就一定要【提问】吗？`

放回你这里：

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

- 一个更上层的路径
